text: The surgeon performs a robotic repair of a medial recurrence hernia after a mesh-based Lichtenstein repair. The dissection begins laterally, creating a peritoneal flap on the right side through the umbilical ligaments, progressing towards the midline. The intermediate fascia is visible as the tissue is reflected against the sidewall to minimize neuropathic pain.

The surgeon performs a limited lateral dissection, focusing on the intermediate fascia and visceral compartment on both medial and lateral aspects. The preperitoneal fat is identified, followed by the identification and reduction of the pseudosac associated with the hernia.

The surgeon focuses on the retrocubic dissection, aggressively developing the space beyond two centimeters between Cooper's ligament and the bladder. The levator muscles and obturator internus are identified midline. The dissection extends beyond the midline to identify the contralateral Cooper's ligament for mesh fixation.

The surgeon identifies the contralateral Cooper's ligament and performs a medial dissection, developing a lateral plane. The spermatic vessels are reflected to the side wall, and the vas is parietalized. An adequate pocket is created for mesh placement, with the iliopsoas muscle identified.

The surgeon examines the anatomy, identifying the lacunar ligament and medial hernia, while observing the indirect space and iliopubic tract. The surgeon rules out a lipoma and assesses the parietalization and retropubic dissection. A mid-weight mesh is used, secured at both ipsilateral and contralateral Cooper's ligaments. The surgeon leaves knots as air knots unless bleeding occurs, to prevent mesh deformation. The peritoneal flap is closed from lateral to medial, with the lateral aspect under more tension and the medial aspect having more redundancy.
The surgeon addresses a small fenestration in the preperitoneal space during a standard repair procedure.
